title: Government budgets revenue and expenditures
description: An exercise providing students with an opportunity to apply their understanding of government budgets; revenue and expenditures.

assessment_items.question_content:
**For the following, pick the most appropriate tax structure from the dropdown choices.**

In a [[☃ dropdown 1]] tax structure, individuals with higher incomes pay a larger percentage of their income in taxes than those with lower incomes.
hints: This tax structure is designed to have those who earn more pay a higher share of their income in taxes.
As income increases, so does the percentage of income paid in taxes.
This system is often seen as more equitable because those with greater ability to pay contribute more.
In a $\boxed{\text{progressive}}$ tax structure, individuals with higher incomes pay a larger percentage of their income in taxes than those with lower incomes.

assessment_items.question_content:
**For the following, pick the most appropriate tax structure from the dropdown choices.**

In a [[☃ dropdown 1]] tax structure, all individuals pay the same percentage of their income, regardless of how much they earn.
hints: This tax structure applies the same tax rate to all income levels.
The percentage of income paid in taxes does not change, regardless of earnings.
This system is sometimes referred to as a "flat tax" because everyone pays the same rate.
In a $\boxed{\text{proportional}}$ tax structure, all individuals pay the same percentage of their income, regardless of how much they earn.

assessment_items.question_content:
**For the following, pick the most appropriate tax structure from the dropdown choices.**

In a [[☃ dropdown 1]] tax structure, individuals with lower incomes pay a larger percentage of their income in taxes than those with higher incomes.
hints: This tax structure places a higher burden on lower-income earners compared to higher-income earners.
The percentage of income paid in taxes decreases as income increases.
Sales taxes are often considered an example of this tax structure.
In a $\boxed{\text{regressive}}$ tax structure, individuals with lower incomes pay a larger percentage of their income in taxes than those with higher incomes.

assessment_items.question_content:
Government revenue is the money collected from various sources, such as taxes, fees, and fines, to fund public services and infrastructure.

**What is the main source of revenue for the federal government?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

Government revenue is the money collected from various sources, such as taxes, fees, and fines, to fund public services and infrastructure.

**What is the main source of revenue for most state governments?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

Government revenue is the money collected from various sources, such as taxes, fees, and fines, to fund public services and infrastructure.

**What is the main source of revenue for local governments?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

Government spending pays for public services, infrastructure, and social programs. It can be mandatory or discretionary.

**Which of the following is an example of mandatory federal spending?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

Government spending pays for public services, infrastructure, and social programs. It can be mandatory or discretionary.

**Which of the following is an example of discretionary federal spending?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

Government spending funds public services, infrastructure, and social programs.

**Which of the following is a significant part of state and local government spending?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

The government budget and national debt are interconnected, as budget changes can directly impact national debt levels.

**How does a budget deficit affect the national debt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The national debt will only increase if the budget deficit is not offset by a surplus the following year.
The national debt decreases immediately whenever there is a budget deficit.
The budget deficit will reduce the national debt over time.
The national debt and budget deficit are the same thing and increase together.

assessment_items.question_content:
The government budget and national debt are interconnected, as budget changes can directly impact national debt levels.

**How does a budget surplus affect the national debt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The national debt will decrease only if the surplus is used to pay down existing debt.
The national debt will decrease automatically whenever there is a budget surplus.
A budget surplus increases the national debt because the government borrows less money.
A budget surplus does not affect the national debt, as they are completely separate.

assessment_items.question_content:
The government budget and national debt are interconnected, as budget changes can directly impact national debt levels.

**How does a balanced budget affect the national debt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A balanced budget means the national debt is automatically paid off.
A balanced budget prevents the national debt from increasing, but it does not eliminate existing debt.
A balanced budget allows the government to borrow more money to reduce the national debt.
A balanced budget means the national debt will increase unless spending is cut.
